summary: DHH argues that software should deliberately embed opinions about how it should be used, rather than trying to offer maximum flexibility. He responds to criticism of Basecamp's design decision to limit todo item responsibility to one person, framing opinionated software as a feature rather than a flaw. Using Ward Cunningham's wiki design and Ruby on Rails as examples, he shows how deliberate constraints can produce better outcomes. He contends that software can never truly be neutral—bias is always present—so builders should embrace and make their opinions explicit. This clarity helps users quickly identify whether software fits their worldview or doesn't.
key_points: Software that limits features based on the builder's beliefs isn't arrogant—it's a deliberate design philosophy that produces better outcomes
Ward Cunningham's wiki deliberately stripped ownership attribution, which fostered community collaboration and made Wikipedia possible
Software can never be truly neutral, just as journalism can never be fully objective—bias is always embedded, so it should be made explicit
Rails' strong opinions are a major driver of its adoption: the closer your views align with its philosophy, the more perfectly it fits
Opinionated software makes mismatches immediately clear, saving users from discovering incompatibility later
money_quotes: Neither Basecamp nor Rails is trying to be all things to all people. Because you can't.
I'm asking for more art and less science in software.
Kind of like the notion of "objective journalism", the bias can never be rooted out.
Ruby on Rails is a tour de force of opinions packed up as software.
When I adopt software, I'm not just looking for features, I'm looking for approaches too.
The closer your opinions on web-application development are to mine, the more Rails will feel like the perfect fit. A fit that couldn't be attained from a neutral position.
Instead of discovering the mismatch of bias down the road.
key_insight: Software is never neutral—embedding strong opinions makes it better for those who share them and honestly signals incompatibility to those who don't.
